title: GenAI System Design
description: Design problems for enterprise assistants, document intelligence, AI search, coding assistants, memory, and evaluation platforms.

questions.title: Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents.
questions.scenario: An enterprise product engineering group runs large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. A recent review found that the design solves the demo but misses scale, permissions, observability, and evaluation; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents measured by answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
questions.expectedAnswer: Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the practical approach is requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations. Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents addresses that risk through requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels. Implement the change with Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.

commonMistakes: Explaining Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents as a memorized definition without tying it to a production failure mode.
Skipping measurement and relying on anecdotal demo quality instead of answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
Ignoring the main risk: demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: An enterprise product engineering group applied Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents in large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. The baseline suffered from demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, then evaluated the change against answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Design around secure RAG platform.
questions.scenario: An enterprise product engineering group runs large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. A recent review found that the design solves the demo but misses scale, permissions, observability, and evaluation; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for secure RAG platform measured by answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
questions.expectedAnswer: secure RAG platform needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the practical approach is retrieval grounding with chunking, hybrid search, reranking, citations, and faithfulness checks. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using Milvus or pgvector, OpenSearch BM25, a cross-encoder reranker, and Ragas-style evaluation, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is retrieving plausible but unsupported context; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.

Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.

questions.title: Design around multi-agent research assistant.
questions.scenario: An enterprise product engineering group runs large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. A recent review found that the design solves the demo but misses scale, permissions, observability, and evaluation; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for multi-agent research assistant measured by answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
questions.expectedAnswer: multi-agent research assistant needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the practical approach is bounded planning, typed tool calls, state management, approval gates, and trace-based evaluation. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using LangGraph, tool schemas, ServiceNow/Jira APIs, and LangSmith traces, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is unbounded autonomy, tool misuse, loops, and hidden state corruption; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Ignoring the main risk: demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations.
Not discussing ownership, rollout, monitoring, and rollback when the approach fails in production.
followUpQuestions: What metric would prove that contract review assistant improved the system rather than just changed it?
What failure mode would you expect after deploying contract review assistant, and how would you detect it?
How would your answer change if latency, cost, or compliance became the primary constraint?
questions.realWorldExample: An enterprise product engineering group applied contract review assistant in large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. The baseline suffered from demo-oriented design that ignores scale, permissions, cost, or operations, causing roughly 25% of reviewed cases to require manual correction. The team implemented requirements-driven architecture with clear data flow, failure handling, observability, security, and rollout using Kafka, Kubernetes, Redis, model registries, gateways, and OpenTelemetry, then evaluated the change against answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption. After a limited rollout, manual corrections dropped by 12-18 percentage points and p95 latency stayed within the agreed operating budget.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate gives a concrete mechanism for contract review assistant, names the data and metrics needed to validate it, and explains the operational risk. A great candidate also discusses rollout, observability, ownership, and when the added complexity is not justified.

questions.title: Design around LLM gateway platform.
questions.scenario: An enterprise product engineering group runs large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation. A recent review found that the design solves the demo but misses scale, permissions, observability, and evaluation; the system must support 50k daily users with p95 latency under 2.5 seconds, so the team needs a reliable approach for LLM gateway platform measured by answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
questions.expectedAnswer: LLM gateway platform needs an end-to-end design that covers requirements, data flow, serving path, evaluation, and operations. In large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the practical approach is model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability. The first step is to establish a baseline with representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels, because without a baseline the team cannot distinguish real improvement from a better-looking demo. Next, implement the smallest production-shaped change using OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping enough trace data to inspect inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures. The main risk is fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems; a strong design makes that risk visible through tests, monitoring, and review workflows. Validation should use answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption, segmented by important user or data groups so averages do not hide regressions. The tradeoff is that stronger controls add complexity, latency, and ownership work, but they also reduce silent failure in production. A good interview answer explains the mechanism, the implementation path, the measurable success criteria, and the condition under which a simpler baseline is preferable.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: The scenario points to fluent output that hides uncertainty, cost, or latency problems. LLM gateway platform addresses that risk through model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability, so the discussion should start with the observed production failure rather than a textbook definition. The first concrete step is to turn recent failures into a benchmark and compare the baseline against the proposed change using answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption.
answerFramework.howToApply: Start with 50-200 representative examples from documents, user actions, conversations, tool results, traces, and feedback labels. Implement the change with OpenAI/Azure OpenAI, vLLM, LiteLLM, model gateways, and eval datasets, keeping the old path available as a baseline. Log inputs, intermediate decisions, outputs, latency, and failures so the team can debug regressions. Roll out behind a feature flag or pilot group, then expand only if quality and operational metrics improve.
answerFramework.example: Example: in large-scale GenAI product with ingestion, retrieval, generation, tools, memory, and evaluation, the baseline path accepts an input, produces a plausible output, but leaves no reliable evidence that the decision is correct. The improved path adds model behavior control through decoding, grounding, evaluation, routing, and observability; it records the evidence, score, trace, or review status that supports the output. The final result is tied to validated signals instead of model confidence or notebook accuracy alone.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: The technique adds engineering overhead, evaluation work, and usually some latency or infrastructure cost. It is worthwhile when the failure affects trust, safety, revenue, or repeated manual effort. It is not worth overengineering for rare, low-risk tasks where a simple baseline already meets the target. Monitor answer quality, availability, latency, cost, safety incidents, and user adoption so the team can tell whether the extra complexity pays for itself.
